data.title: VPLS: Another Look At This Solid, Vanilla Vanguard Bond ETF
data.description:
The Vanguard Core-Plus Bond ETF (VPLS) is an actively managed ETF focusing on simple investment-grade bonds, treasuries, and MBS, with smaller investments in foreign and high-yield bonds. VPLS is a simple, strong, vanilla bond ETF with a well-balanced portfolio, an above-average 4.5% dividend yield, and consistent outperformance since inception, and is a buy. (Our last article on it was here).

VPLS: Overview And Analysis

Strategy And Portfolio

VPLS is an actively managed bond ETF by Vanguard. Although the company is more well-known for its index ETFs, it does have a couple active offerings, and that includes VPLS.

The fund's active management could lead to higher income and returns moving forward, contingent on its managers making effective decisions. Active management is something of a risk factor too, as managers might make ineffective, unprofitable decisions too. Active ETFs have the highest potential returns, but index ETFs are generally safer, so focusing on these is always a reasonable choice.

Although VPLS is active, it is quite similar to its benchmark or index, the Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND). There are some important differences, but the broad strokes are the same.

VPLS focuses on high-quality, investment-grade securities, with treasuries and MBS accounting for just under half of its portfolio. Most of its other half consists of quality investment-grade corporate bonds, with smaller, single-digit investments in high-yield bonds. Allocations are as follows:

Diversification is quite good, due to the above and due to the fund investing in over 2,000 different bonds. BND invests in over 10,000, which increases diversification somewhat, although at these levels the impact is marginal: thousands of securities are more than enough to ensure a diversified portfolio. At the same time, VPLS's portfolio is a bit broader than that of BND, broader than most other bond ETFs too, which means higher diversification too.

Risk And Volatility

As mentioned previously, VPLS focuses on high-quality securities, with smaller investments in riskier high-yield bonds. Allocations are as follows:

VPLS's credit quality is quite strong, which should minimize drawdowns during downturns and recessions. It is possible for the fund to see gains during these, due to a flight-to-quality effect impacting its treasuries and MBS, and as the Federal Reserve tends to cut rates during recessions, putting upward pressure on quality bond prices.

VPLS's credit quality is somewhat weaker than that of its benchmark, which does not invest in high-yield bonds in any meaningful amount.

Due to the above, I would expect VPLS to slightly underperform its benchmark during most downturns and recessions. Active management could reduce any drawdowns here, perhaps even lead to outperformance, although that is not my base case scenario. Losses and underperformance should both be quite minor, as the differences in credit quality here are not all that significant.

With its inception in late 2023, the fund has yet to experience a meaningful downturn. Closest scenario to this is the equity bear market in early 2025, caused by bearish sentiment/the imposition of tariffs. Equity market losses barely impacted fixed-income markets, although there were small, temporary losses there, and VPLS did seem to underperform. Results are barely significant, short-lived, and could plausibly be the result of normal volatility. Results are as follows:

VPLS's rate risk is moderate, with a duration of 5.6 years, not significantly different from BND's 5.7 figure. Expect moderate gains if rates were to plummet and losses if these were to increase. Do remember that VPLS is exposed to treasury, MBS, and corporate bond rates, not Federal Reserve rates per se. Significant changes to Fed rates would almost certainly have an impact on rates for these securities; smaller changes might not. As mentioned previously, VPLS is a young fund and has yet to experience a full rate cycle, so I can't really gauge its performance during one.

VPLS's credit risk is low, and rate risk is moderate. Compared to its benchmark, it has a bit more credit risk, but the differences are not all that significant. At the same time, it is more diversified on an asset class basis, which should decrease risks somewhat. Active management might do so too, although this is far from certain.

Overall, I would expect VPLS's realized volatility and drawdowns to both be slightly or marginally higher than those of its benchmark. Realized volatility seems, if anything, below average. Results here seem slightly above expectations but don't seem too surprising; see the above, and as the differences are small.

Circling back, VPLS's overall risk and volatility seem low and about average compared to its benchmark. The former is an important benefit for shareholders, the second an important consideration for the same. Active ETFs are generally riskier than its index peers, but that does not seem to be the case for VPLS, and it does merit mention.

Dividend Yield And Performance

Active bond ETFs generally sport above-average dividend yields, at least relative to their benchmarks, as their managers focus on higher-yielding securities. VPLS's 4.5% dividend yield is higher than that of its benchmark, as expected.

VPLS's dividend yield is marginally higher than that of investment-grade corporate bond benchmark ETFs, even though credit quality is arguably higher (significantly higher treasury/MBS allocations should outweigh the smaller investments in high-yield bonds, but these comparisons are not trivial to do).

Active bond ETFs might outperform their benchmarks, contingent on their managers picking the right securities and making effective decisions. Results here are much more uncertain than for the dividends because it is trivially easy to pick higher-yielding securities much harder to pick outperforming securities. VPLS has outperformed its benchmark since inception, and reasonably consistently so. Technically it has underperformed these past thirty days, but only marginally so, and the difference seems unsustainable from volatility.

Well-Balanced Advantages And Investment Thesis

Throughout the article, I've shown that VPLS's dividend yield and past returns are higher than those of its benchmark, with realized volatility comparable. Although the fund does not excel in any of these three metrics, it does do reasonably well on each, making for an incredibly well-rounded fund and investment thesis. Lots of advantages, fewer disadvantages, none significant.

VPLS - Other Considerations

I mentioned before that VPLS is quite close to its benchmark, without any significant differences. An important implication of this is that the fund's performance should, ultimately, be quite close to that of its benchmark. Slight outperformance and underperformance are both possible, and the former has occurred since inception, but significant differences in performance seem all but impossible. This is not the case for lots of ETFs, including the Eldridge BBB-B CLO ETF (CLOZ) and the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond Buywrite Strategy ETF (TLTW), two I've previously covered.

Due to the above, more aggressive investors might prefer other funds. Something like TLTW could easily significantly outperform from here on out, on account of its massive 13.5% distribution yield. It could easily underperform too, on account of its massive 15.4-year duration.

Flipside of the above is that more conservative, risk-averse investments might prefer VPLS over its peers. This is particularly the case for more conservative medium- and long-term investors; short-term investors should probably focus on cash, as well as those desiring market-like returns.

Conclusion

VPLS's well-balanced portfolio and investment thesis, above-average 4.5% dividend yield, and consistent outperformance make the fund a buy. As the fund focuses on high-quality securities and does not significantly differ from its benchmark, it might be of particular interest to more risk-averse investors.

data.title: Federal Realty Trust Q4 Earnings: Malls Are Thriving
data.description:
Consistency is rare in the real estate game. A recent theme of my coverage in Seeking Alpha has been the cyclicality of real estate. I’ve recently talked about companies like Camden Property Trust (CPT) and Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN), highlighting how each is navigating cyclicality in their own ways. While both are struggling in the face of tailwinds, both have pivoted and adapted to a changing environment with differing degrees of success. Even more recently, I discussed Modiv Industrial (MDV), focusing on their recent hinting at a take-private after failing to achieve their value in the public markets. The point being, times are tough in the world of real estate.

With an increasing number of landlords sitting in the doldrums, REITs that have been able to charge forward are few and far between. Even more rare are companies that are able to stick to their core business in the face of an ever-changing world. Perhaps the best representative example of this paradigm is Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT). FRT is one of the nation’s largest mall landlords and one of the few companies to achieve a 50-year streak of rising dividends.

FRT’s reputation as the dividend king of real estate makes the company one of the more well-known publicly traded REITs. Today, we will follow up on my prior coverage of FRT and discuss 2025 earnings.

Review of Prior Coverage

Last year, I initiated coverage on FRT. This article included an overview of the REIT’s portfolio, business model, and capitalization. Today, the company has continued much down the same path, so I will keep the review brief.

FRT is one of the largest dedicated owner operators of shopping center real estate in the United States. The company owns more than 100 properties covering around 27 million square feet of retail space and over 3,000 units of multifamily. In total, the footprint of FRT's portfolio covers more than 2,100 acres. As you would expect with any shopping center owner, tenancy is diverse with more than 3,500 commercial tenants.

My last coverage of FRT focused around the operating business model of mall REITs. More specifically, I talked about how being a mall landlord is much closer to running a traditional business than running a triple net REIT like Agree Realty (ADC). The business is more capital intensive and requires a more hands-on approach. Margins are more sensitive to changes in the cost structure, and there are more opportunities to generate revenue through ancillary sources like parking and advertising revenue. While this highlights opportunity for value add via creativity, it opens the REIT to a host of new issues. Suddenly, short-term changes in consumer preference and demand become impactful to the REIT’s top and bottom lines.

For FRT, this means a business that is inherently less predictable and more susceptible to year-over-year change. From this perspective, FRT’s track record of rising dividends is even more unusual. For instance, compare FRT’s dividend payments to competitors like Simon Property Group (SPG) or Macerich (MAC).

My prior coverage also outlined how FRT has managed to benefit from the other side of real estate’s cyclicality. Since publication, FRT has performed well on solid retail demand. FRT has outperformed real estate (VNQ) and the S&P 500.

Earnings Review

FRT recently reported full-year earnings for 2025. On the back of remarkable strength in the shopping center market, FRT delivered remarkably strong results. Top-line and per-share results grew significantly, and the company delivered some encouraging tidbits around their business. Management gave their feedback on the earnings call.

Strong quarter, strong year, strong 2026 guidance, 6.4% bottom line FFO growth in the quarter, 4.3% for the year and guidance close to 6% at the midpoint for 2026.

Along the theme of FRT being a REIT with an operational business, it should not surprise us that leasing velocity and activity account for a powerful portion of earnings. While other REITs depend heavily on acquiring or building new assets, a significant portion of FRT’s value is delivered through adding value within their portfolio. Over the year, FRT leased $11 million worth of annualized base rent.

No surprise that leasing drives these and future results. With 601,000 feet of comparable deals done in the quarter at 12% rollover and 2.3 million feet of comparable deals done for the year at 15% rollover, an incremental $11 million of new rent is under contract.

As FRT continues to add value through lease-up and redevelopment of their existing assets, the company also has a value-add component. Right now, FRT is looking to begin engaging one of their smaller, ancillary business units of developing residential at their properties. Right now, FRT is adding multifamily to several assets, including Blayr at Bala Cynwyd.

We've previously disclosed the allocation of a total of $280 million for new residential development of the Blayr at Bala Cynwyd, which is nearly complete and ready for lease-up beginning this quarter, 301 Washington Street, Hoboken and Lot 12 at Santana Row, which together will add more than 500 units to the portfolio. And just this quarter, we've added another residential project to our development schedule that you can see in the 8-K. Willow Grove Shopping Center in suburban Philadelphia will be completely redeveloped and include an additional 261 apartments to complement a modernized and remerchandised shopping center.

Our experience with residential development at our retail-centric properties is a skill set developed over 25 years and is certainly a unique differentiator of our business plan. After enjoying the 6.5% to 7% or higher income contribution from each of these residential additions for a period of time, we have the optionality to take advantage of cap rates well inside those yields and reinvest them tax efficiently, just as we've done so effectively last year and this.

Creativity is the name of the game for FRT. When the sun is shining, FRT needs to make hay and capitalize on tailwinds. If we rewind to five years ago, FRT was in survival mode. Post-pandemic and staring down Amazon (AMZN), the boogeyman of retail. Bankruptcies were booming, and nobody imagined pouring capital into building a new shopping center. Fast forward, and retail made a comeback. The lack of new supply coming online over the past five years means an undersupplied and growing market is helping FRT deliver strong results.

Right now, things look bright, and FRT is making the most of it.

Outlook and Risks

As I mentioned, FRT is a relatively sensitive business for a REIT. The company is forced to manage their portfolio and commit capital on a speculative basis to keep their assets in top shape and add value. This is often difficult for a mall operator who is forced to predict consumer trends and macroeconomic changes simultaneously.

Add in more uncertainty around economic policy like tariffs and trade, and the outlook becomes even more murky. At the end of the day, FRT is powered by people going to their malls and spending money at stores and restaurants. This means economic activity is the main success driver for FRT, like many other companies.

Based on market review from Cushman & Wakefield, the shopping center segment has performed well over the past year.

Retail market fundamentals improved over the course of 2025, driven by limited new supply, robust backfilling activity, and reduced uncertainty around tariffs and consumer spending. These dynamics translated into stronger demand in Q4, as the U.S. economy absorbed 3.4 msf of retail space—the strongest quarterly performance since Q4 2023. After three quarters of weakness, every region posted positive Q4 absorption. The Midwest led with 1.2 msf followed by the West with 1.0 msf, the Northeast with 638,000 sf, and the South with 589,000 sf. Despite this late-year rebound, all regions remained negative for 2025 overall. Shopping center vacancies remain near historic lows. For context, pre-pandemic vacancy rates typically hovered around 7%, making today’s 5.7% especially tight despite absorption volatility experienced throughout 2024 and early 2025. High quality retail space remains scarce in most markets.

Recently, I’ve been highlighting what I see as stress within private credit and private equity. Rising nonaccruals and delayed exits of some of the hotter names have led to some jitters around the near- and mid-term economic outlooks. FRT is a secondary participant in these markets, often depending on sponsored businesses in the retail industry to pay rent. If bankruptcies are to begin appearing and there is another retail-pocalypse, FRT and other mall REITs may find themselves in trouble.

Over the past few years, rising costs have increasingly pressured the consumer. This past holiday season, spending was impacted as families were forced to reconsider their budgeting. Going further, shopping online is still a growing threat as online shopping continues to grow as a portion of total retail sales. However, looking at the big picture, the boogeyman of online shopping has still taken a relatively small bite out of the enormous retail industry.

After all, the threat of AMZN was enough to begin cracking confidence in some of the largest mall landlords.

FRT and similar owners have a degree of insulation through the experiential component of their properties. Going to the mall remains a desirable experience for people across demographic groups, despite volatility in shorter-term trends.

Conclusion

From a long-term perspective, FRT is one of the most well-positioned retail REITs. While their business is inherently capital intensive, popular malls are goldmines for generating percentage rent through rising foot traffic. As FRT continues to reinvest in its assets, the company should be able to stay ahead of the next generation of retail trends. Following a run for FRT shares over several months, the company earns a Hold rating.

data.title: S&P Global: Rattled By Irrational AI Concerns
data.description:
There's been a certain irony facing S&P Global Inc. (SPGI) of late. While three years of solid double-digit gains have left one of its leading products, the S&P 500, looking rather expensive, shares of the index's parent have been headed in the opposite direction, driven lower by fears that AI will wreak havoc on anything connected to software or data.

Those fears have left SPGI around 25% lighter than it was just a few weeks ago. Meanwhile, and despite guiding for a slightly softer 2026 print than the market was hoping for, per-share profit growth is set to remain above 10%. While those contrasting trends haven't been great for SPGI's price-to-earnings ratio, the net result is great for anyone who wants to buy a quality business at a knockdown price. AI fears are worth taking on here, and I rate shares "Strong Buy."

Overdone Data Fears

Following the release of its 2025 results, SPGI's management guided to a midpoint of $19.53 in non-GAAP 2026 EPS. This was just below the circa $20 analysts had been expecting the company to earn pre-results. Cue modest downward revisions to EPS estimates.

While this could conceivably explain some of SPGI's weakness (shares ended the day 10% lower), AI fears are a more likely culprit, not least of all because other financial data stocks have also tumbled recently. One specific trigger was the Feb 3 announcement of a new plugin from startup Anthropic for legal work. SPGI fell around 11% that day. Frankly, the market has been itching to give these firms a clobbering, hence the extreme reaction to SPGI's results.

The company's revenue mix should help assuage concerns. Around 30% comes from the Ratings segment, roughly half of which is transaction-based. Think of fees from providing ratings to fresh corporate or government debt, for example.

Though cyclical, one attractive feature of this business is that it offers natural "GDP-plus" revenue growth. That is to say, volumes should grow above or in line with economic growth, with price hikes providing the "plus" component. That's been the case historically, with segmental revenues growing at a circa 7% CAGR this past decade.

Another thing to like here is that the actual cost of getting a rating is a relatively small component of total issuance costs. We're talking less than 10 basis points. This leads to less blowback when agencies like SPGI raise their rates by 3%-4%. Given high regulatory and other barriers to entry, AI is not a problem here.

That leaves the company's more data-centric segments: Market Intelligence, Energy, Mobility, Indices, and the non-transaction component of Ratings. Collectively, these account for most of SPGI's revenue.

Much of this data is proprietary and highly specialized. Visible Alpha, for example, is one product that sits in the Market Intelligence unit. A third of this unit's revenue comes from the investment management industry. Asset managers typically want an "edge" for obvious reasons, and access to specialized data is one way to get it. Anyway, Visible Alpha offers very detailed and granular consensus estimates—the kind of estimates that aren't available via Google or Yahoo. With a subscription likely to set you back a five-figure sum, wouldn't that leave it ripe for disruption from a cheaper alternative, perhaps backed by AI? That would be a stretch. Leave aside the fact that much of this data isn't publicly available; cheaper alternatives based on stripped-down offerings exist already. They have to get their data from somewhere, and in many cases that somewhere is S&P Global. Indeed, one of the company's customers is Anthropic, the very same firm that has caused such a splash recently.

The Indices business is another one where it is difficult to see AI landing much of a punch. This unit is home to the iconic S&P 500, and around 65% of its revenue comes from asset-based fees from products like exchange-traded funds.

Much like the Ratings business, growth in this unit should also be above trend, given it benefits from both market appreciation and net inflows. With ETFs holding over $5 trillion in assets linked to an S&P Global-owned index, it is difficult to see this business going anywhere soon.

In total, only 5% of SPGI's revenue is derived from truly non-proprietary publicly available data. In other words, the kind that is legitimately vulnerable to AI.

Quality Business, Knockdown Price

With the market seemingly fixated on AI, the fundamentals of SPGI's business are being overlooked. There are two things I like in particular. One is the firm's propensity for margin expansion, which is a function of its largely fixed cost base. SPGI's operating margin was 50.4% last year, a 140 bps increase on 2024. The second is the lack of capital intensity in its business. SPGI's balance sheet is very light on physical assets, so its returns on tangible capital are stratospheric. This results in lots of free cash flow, which has been great for shareholder distributions.

With over 50 years of consecutive hikes behind it, SPGI is a bona fide Dividend King. Buybacks have also been plentiful, with the company taking out 3% of its shares just last year.

The above is critical to understanding the value proposition here. At $410, SPGI trades for 23 times last year's adjusted EPS. While it may not look like your conventional value stock, there's only a limited need for SPGI to reinvest in its underlying operations, meaning all the "E" in its P/E ratio is available for things like dividends, buybacks, and M&A. That alone is worth a premium, and it's partly why the stock has historically commanded an above-market price-to-earnings ratio. Between the start of 2016 and the end of 2025, for example, SPGI averaged roughly 30 times trailing non-GAAP earnings.

That puts today's buyer in a nice position. Analysts are forecasting double-digit EPS growth over the next few years. Including its dividend, SPGI offers a circa 13% annualized return from its business. Shares should re-rate higher once AI fears dissipate, leading to an even greater investment return. If not, well, a 13% CAGR is still healthy enough. With an attractive risk/reward proposition, short-sighted AI fears are well worth taking on here. I rate S&P Global "Strong Buy."

data.title: Amazon: After Nine Consecutive Days Of Declines, History Suggests Backing Up The Truck
data.description:
I last wrote about Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), in June 2025, and I was bullish. Amazon remains one of my favorite stocks, especially after the shares plunged by about 20% due to increased capital expenditure spending plans announced with the Q4 earnings release. CEO Andy Jassy said the company plans to spend about $200 billion in 2026 on AI and other capital expenditures. This has caused some investors to worry about the impact on Amazon's free cash flow, which is now likely to be negative in 2026 if these capex goals are reached. However, I see the sell first, ask questions later trading approach in the market, particularly with Amazon and many other tech stocks, as being shortsighted.

I believe all these concerns, combined with an overall pullback in many tech stocks, present an ideal buying opportunity, especially for investors with a longer-term view. While I could understand a small pullback in Amazon shares due to the impact that increased capex spending will have on free cash flow this year, I believe a 20% decline from the recent highs is way overdone. While short-sighted investors and short sellers focus on this year's declining free cash flow, I will focus on Amazon's operating cash flow, which remains as high as ever. Furthermore, Amazon has numerous future growth drivers and growing dominance in many major markets. With all this in mind, let's take an updated look at Amazon.

The Chart

As shown below, Amazon shares traded for nearly $250 just weeks ago. However, the stock has plunged by just over 20% from these recent highs and recently traded for a bit below $200. This stock now trades well below the 50-day moving average, which is around $230 per share, and also below the 200-day moving average, which is around $223 per share. If this stock doesn't rebound soon, a bearish "death cross" could form on the chart if the 50-day moving average crosses below the 200-day moving average.

This might be weighing on some investors who may be expecting further weakness; however, I would not be surprised to see this stock rebound back towards the 200-day moving average, which could reduce the chance of this bearish formation appearing on the chart. I want to point out that this stock has not been this low since the aftermath of the "Liberation Day" tariff plunge that occurred in April 2025. Within a couple of months of the Liberation Day market plunge, this stock rebounded sharply, and I believe another sharp rebound could be coming.

Let's Compare Amazon To Walmart

Amazon is involved in so many high-growth areas of the market, and this company could continue to dominate in many areas in the future. This company has major market share when it comes to retail, with about 38% of e-commerce in the United States going through Amazon.com. This is a massive lead since the second largest e-commerce retailer, Walmart (WMT), has just 6.4% of the market. It's worth noting that Amazon is showing growing dominance in other areas as well. Amazon Web Services, or "AWS," has about 30% of the market when it comes to cloud services. Amazon also has about 75% of the retail media ad spending market. With all of its businesses, I see Amazon as having a growing network effect that could become more powerful over time. This "ecosystem" and its scale also create a wide moat.

In terms of valuation, Amazon is expected to earn nearly $8 per share in 2026, which suggests a price-to-earnings ratio of about 25. Furthermore, analysts expect Amazon to post annual earnings growth of about 20% on average for the next few years. By contrast, Walmart is expected to earn about $2.64 per share in 2026, which implies a price-to-earnings ratio of about 51. This is about twice Amazon's price-to-earnings ratio, representing a huge valuation gap. Furthermore, Walmart is only expected to see annual earnings growth of about 12% on average for the next 3 years or so. This growth rate is significantly below that of Amazon's, which again suggests a massive valuation gap. Of course, Amazon has much more than just the retail side of its business, but the retail is a huge part, and when I can buy Amazon at half the price-to-earnings multiple while it also has a much higher growth rate, I believe that is the smart choice.

Amazon's Advertising Business

While investors know Amazon well for its retail division and for AWS (cloud services), fewer investors are aware of or focus on the advertising business it has developed. This is in spite of the fact that Amazon has one of the largest advertising businesses in the world. Amazon generates ad revenues from sponsored ads on its e-commerce site and in other areas such as Prime Video. The advertising business has posted growth rates of about 22% recently, and that high level of growth is expected to continue for years. Amazon's advertising business has big profit margins, and some analysts believe its revenues could double to around $140 billion between now and 2030.

Robotics And Prime Membership Price Increases Could Drive Higher Margins In 2026

Amazon could be a huge beneficiary of AI and robotics, and I don't believe this potential is fully priced into the stock right now. This company has significant potential to expand margins by increasing its use of AI and robots rather than relying on human labor. Amazon recently announced more layoffs at levels described as "staggering." I believe these layoffs will continue and widen profit margins for the company. I also believe profit margins can expand as Amazon raises prices over time. Analysts expect the company to raise membership fees for "Prime" sometime in 2026. Prime offers members free and expedited shipping, plus a host of other benefits, including streaming. Analysts project the company could raise Prime membership fees from $139 to $159 annually. This is still far below the estimated $1,430 in annual benefits the membership provides.

Moonshots

Amazon has some "moonshots" that it is developing, and this includes Zoox, which is a robotaxi that recently launched in Las Vegas and San Francisco. It plans to rapidly expand this robotaxi service, aiming to produce 10,000 robotaxi units per year. The next expansion is set for Austin, Texas, and Miami, Florida. As shown below, the robotaxi market is expected to see rapid growth for many years and potentially reach a market size of about $450 billion by 2033. If Zoox captures even a small percentage of this market, it could substantially increase revenues.

Another moonshot is "Project Kuiper," now known as "Amazon Leo," which is Amazon's satellite Internet system poised to compete with Starlink. This could create a major new recurring revenue stream and further strengthen the company's network effect or ecosystem. Amazon Leo will provide low-latency connectivity for customers in remote areas. This could bring a billion people online in places like India and Africa, potentially creating new shoppers for its e-commerce site. Some analyst projections suggest this service could reach 100 million subscribers paying $30 per month, which could generate $36 billion in annual revenue.

Move Over Nvidia, Amazon's AI Chips Are Here

Amazon has been developing AI chips such as "Trainium" and "Graviton," which now have a combined annual run rate of more than $10 billion. The numbers would likely be even higher without supply constraints. Amazon appears poised to keep developing this fast-growing segment, with Trainium 4 chips expected to be released in 2027. These chips will offer 6 times the compute performance, four times more memory bandwidth, and 2 times more high memory bandwidth capacity than Trainium 3. Graviton 5 is Amazon Web Services' most powerful chip for cloud workloads, offering speed and efficiency with competitive pricing.

During the Q4 earnings call, CEO Andy Jassy announced the company would spend about $200 billion in capital expenditures to capture major opportunities that he sees in AI, chips, robotics, and low earth orbit satellites. The market reacted negatively as the stock declined significantly over concerns about excessive spending. The decline didn't make any sense to me, especially since Andy Jassy said that Amazon is "monetizing capacity as fast as they can install it." Amazon's AI chips are expected to reduce costs and reliance on Nvidia chips going forward. Furthermore, I see Amazon's AI chips as another piece of Amazon's growing ecosystem.

Here's One More Big Reason Why I Am Backing Up The Truck

Aside from the much lower valuation and the fact that this stock hasn't traded below $200 since the aftermath of the Liberation Day tariffs, there is another reason why historical analysis suggests it is time to back up the truck. It's worth noting that Amazon shares have recently dropped for 9 consecutive days due to capital expenditure spending fears, and this (9 consecutive days of declines) has not occurred since July 2006. I see this as another sign that this is a rare buying opportunity because the chance to buy this stock after 9 days of declines hasn't happened in about 2 decades. I also want to point out that investors who sold Amazon during the July 2006 selloff and investors who were afraid to buy during that selloff missed out on a big opportunity. Amazon shares surged by about 128% in the following 12 months. The reason Amazon shares plunged in July 2006 was that the company announced capex spending on Prime (which had been launched just a year before) and other infrastructure investments, which, as we all know, ended up significantly rewarding investors.

Potential Risk Factors To Consider

I believe the risks of a recession are increasing, which could potentially impact Amazon's retail and advertising business in a very negative way. AI appears to be causing numerous job losses, and there is a chance this gets worse in the coming years and leads us to recession. Many consumers already appear to be struggling, and this has led to weak consumer sentiment numbers. The New York Fed recently reported that consumer delinquencies have reached a 10-year high, and this could get worse in the event of more job losses or a recession. In addition to these macro concerns, Amazon could face challenges from regulatory issues and an FTC lawsuit, which is expected to go to trial in October 2026, which could force the sale of AWS or the advertising business. Amazon also continues to face competitive challenges and the potential risk of overspending on capex.

In Summary

There has been a general "risk off" sentiment in the markets lately, and this seems to have exacerbated what I view as an excessive decline in the shares of Amazon stock. I believe this is a major buying opportunity, and it is the biggest pullback we have seen in this stock since the Liberation Day market plunge. I believe this stock will rebound toward the 200-day moving average, which is around $223, fairly soon. Amazon continues to show revenue growth and increasing dominance in retail, advertising, and cloud services. In addition, it has moonshots, which could become major growth drivers in the future.

While some investors fret about capex spending plans, even as the CEO has suggested this spending will lead to immediate monetization, I will focus on the fact that operating cash flow remains strong and free cash flow will likely rebound after the capex investments are completed. For all these reasons, I rate Amazon as a Strong Buy and I am backing up the truck now while the stock is on sale.

data.title: How REITs Became The Safe Haven Trade In The Tech Wreck
data.description:
Well, it took longer than I expected, but REITs are finally back in favor. As measured by the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ), REITs are up 6.9% year to date against a broader market that is otherwise down.

The inflection point was really the start of February, as the former hot trades started to reverse. Earnings season brought greater focus to the extreme amount of capex spending being committed by the hyperscalers.

While AI is proving to be incredibly powerful, it is also massively expensive, to the tune of trillions of dollars invested by just a few entities. The full expense is not fully showing up in their earnings statements due to delayed depreciation accounting elections, but as more and more analysts are shining a light on the full capex numbers, the market has started to revalue the stocks.

While extremely high earnings multiples appeared justified by the potential earnings of AI products, the capex suggests margins might be lower than previously anticipated such that valuations needed to come down. Falling market prices were widespread in February.

Cryptocurrency imploded. Microsoft, Google, and Amazon are down 5%, 10%, and 18%, respectively. Software companies like Salesforce (CRM) are being increasingly threatened by replacement through AI.

All these formerly hot areas form the bulk of the market, so as they fall, the indices fall. The Nasdaq is down 4% in the first 2 weeks of February, while the slightly more diversified S&P is down 2%.

Traditional Safe Havens Have Not Worked

Most of the traditional safe haven plays have not worked either. Many consider these megacaps to be the safe haven among equities. Gold and silver fell from historic highs:

Even bonds have been spotty as the long end of the yield curve has remained relatively high.

Instead, people are turning to REITs as the safe haven.

Why REITs Represent Safety Right Now

We see 4 reasons why REITs make a great place to hide and even grow through the market reset.

  1. Fundamental strength
  2. Reliable cashflows
  3. Valuation
  4. Directly benefit from the tech capex

Fundamental Strength

REITs, at a fundamental level, have been performing well for quite some time. Here are the vital statistics of the last 5 years.

  • Margins improved from 39.6% to 42.2%
  • Debt was reduced with Equity to assets increasing from 43.9% to 44.8%
  • FFO grew each of the last 5 years

Reliable Cash Flows

Most REIT revenue is contractual in nature. Depending on the property sector, these contracts can extend as long as 30 years. That sort of visibility into future cash flows provides a degree of safety in an otherwise turbulent market. REITs use these cash flows to pay out dividends, which have largely been growing. 36 REITs are forecast to increase their dividends in the first quarter of 2026.

As contractual cash flows are inherent to the structure of most property sectors, these reliable dividends are not a new feature. However, the factor that makes them stand out right now is the size of cash flows compared to what you can get in the broader market.

REIT dividends are particularly big relative to the rest of the market because of an atypical delta in valuation.

REIT Relative Value

REITs have historically traded at FFO multiples that are roughly in line with S&P earnings multiples.

In recent years there has been a divergence, with REIT multiples declining while S&P multiples ballooned.

As of 2/12/26, the median REIT trades at 13.8X FFO. The S&P trades at 29.3X trailing earnings.

That is nearly double the historically “normal” multiple of about 15X.

Earnings yield and FFO yield are the inverse of multiples. So that means that REITs have an FFO yield of 7.24%, while the S&P has an earnings yield of 3.4%

With more than double the earnings power of the S&P, REITs are able to pay out much larger dividends. Traditionally REITs are a dividend-focused sector, so they almost always have higher yields than the S&P, but due to the current spread in valuation, the dividend spread is much larger.

  • S&P dividend yield: 1.15%
  • REIT dividend yield: 3.8% (Dow Jones Equity REIT Index)

Both yields are as of 2/12/26.

The REIT index yield is dragged down by some of the large caps, such as Welltower (WELL) (the biggest REIT), trading at very high multiples.

If one buys individual REITs instead of ETFs, much higher yields can be attained without even having to stretch much further out on the risk spectrum. 2CHYP, the portfolio of Portfolio Income Solutions, has a yield of 5.6%. We have client accounts with yields well over 8%.

The point is that with REITs broadly trading at cheap valuations and the broader market trading at very high valuations, the delta in cash flow yield and dividends is much larger than normal.

During the inflation portion of the AI bubble, the market didn’t seem to care about the higher yield of REITs. A few extra percentage points just didn’t feel like enough when compared to the seemingly infinite promise of AI.

As time progresses, AI still looks powerful, but the reports about trillions of dollars of capex have shown that there is also a cost side of AI. It can certainly still lead to strong growth, but maybe it isn’t worth paying extreme multiples for it.

The market seems to be remembering that value does indeed matter. With valuation back in the calculus, the relatively undervalued REITs are outperforming.

Directly Benefit From AI Capex

A significant number of REITs and other infrastructure plays, which were previously overlooked, directly benefit from the capex being spent on AI.

The trillions of dollars being spent by hyperscalers on AI infrastructure might be a cost problem for them, but the suppliers of this infrastructure are clear beneficiaries.

Regular, boring electric utilities are not so boring anymore as data centers drive massive demand. Recent earnings reports revealed:

  • Duke Energy (DUK) has a pipeline of over $100B
  • American Electric Power (AEP) doubled its committed load forecast through 2030.

REITs are also seeing direct benefit. In its recent earnings report, Equinix (EQIX) demonstrated accelerating AFFO/share growth from a combination of lease-up at existing data centers and high ROIC development of new data centers.

The stocks are responding.

The Power of Being Oversold in an Overbought Market

What we are witnessing in this tech wreck is not an abject failure of tech. Fundamentally, most of the crashing companies are still doing fairly well.

It is simply a correction of what was previously an extreme valuation. Capital is leaving the bloated sectors and pouring into the overlooked areas of the market.

REITs are the same steady cash flow companies they have been for decades. Certain supply and demand dynamics make the outlook marginally stronger than normal, but the real reason they are the safe haven in the tech crash is because they are trading at attractive valuations relative to the cash flows they provide.

The outperformance of REITs observed so far in 2026 is, in my opinion, just the tip of the iceberg. Substantially more outperformance will be needed to normalize relative value.

data.title: FXU: Utilities Dashboard For February
data.description:
This monthly article series aims at a top-down analysis of the utilities sector based on value, quality, and momentum. It may also help to analyze sector ETFs such as Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU) and First Trust Utilities AlphaDEX Fund ETF (FXU), whose largest holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italics describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), and Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on the trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY, and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales, and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or unavailable when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set into a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively as EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, and GMh, and they are calculated as the averages over a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for hardware in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in hardware companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between two valuation ratios (EY, SY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh). FY is reported for consistency with other sector dashboards, but it is ignored in utilities’ scores to avoid some inconsistencies. In the same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the two valuation ratios are of equal importance.

Current Data

The next table shows the metrics and scores as of writing. Columns stand for all the data defined above.

Value and Quality Chart

The next chart plots the Value and Quality scores by industry. Higher is better.

Evolution Since Last Month

Momentum

The next chart plots median returns by subsector.

Interpretation

Water utilities are undervalued by about 15% relative to 11-year averages, while gas utilities are overvalued by 7%, and electric/multi utilities by 17%. Overvaluation is partly justified by a good quality score for the latter.

Focus on FXU

First Trust Utilities AlphaDEX Fund ETF (FXU) was launched on 5/8/2007 and tracks the StrataQuant® Utilities Index. FXU has 40 holdings, a 30-day SEC yield of 2.69%, and an expense ratio of 0.61%, while the capital-weighted ETF XLU charges only 0.08%. As described by First Trust, the underlying index builds a portfolio of stocks with either growth or value characteristics by separately ranking, selecting, and weighting Russell 1000 utilities companies with growth and value metrics.

The portfolio is concentrated, but company-specific risk is moderate. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 40.2% of asset value, and the heaviest position weighs 4.5%. FXU is much more diversified than XLU, whose largest position (Nextera Energy) weighs 13.6%.

Despite FXU’s better diversification and recent outperformance, these funds are equivalents for long-term investors. However, XLU is a better trading instrument due to much higher trading volumes. Investors seeking a balanced portfolio may prefer Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Utilities ETF (RSPU). Additionally, RSPU has a lower fee compared to FXU (0.40%) and has outperformed it since inception, especially over the past decade.

Dashboard List

I use the first table to calculate value and quality scores. It may also be used in a stock-picking process to check how companies stand among their peers. For example, the EY column tells us that a gas company with an earnings yield above 0.0499 (or price/earnings below 20.04) is in the better half of the industry regarding this metric. A dashboard list is sent every month to Quantitative Risk & Value subscribers with the most profitable companies standing in the better half among their peers regarding the three valuation metrics at the same time. The stocks below are part of the list sent to subscribers a few weeks ago.
